task: I got a notice about a claim and I’m trying not to get tangled up in it. Can you help me figure out the usual next steps and what I should keep in writing so I don’t miss anything important?

turns.agent_response:
Yes. Keep it simple and in writing.

### Usual next steps
- Read the notice carefully and mark any **deadline**
- Check what they’re asking for: **documents, photos, estimate, inspection, proof of loss, repair records**
- Reply only with the basics if needed: that you received it and are reviewing it
- If they want something from you, send it **in writing** and keep a copy
- If there’s an inspection or call, write down **date, time, name, company, and what was said**

### What to keep
Save everything:
- The notice itself
- Envelope or email header
- Any claim number
- All emails, letters, texts, and voicemails notes
- Photos and videos
- Repair receipts, invoices, contractor estimates
- Any inventory list or proof of damage
- Notes from phone calls: who, when, what they said

### Good habit
Don’t rely on a phone call. If it matters, get it in writing.

### If you want, I can also help you draft a short reply that says:
- you got the notice
- you’re reviewing it
- you want everything in writing

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. Keep it in writing, sure. But I’d want the exact wording for a reply that doesn’t give them anything extra.

Also — what else should I log besides the obvious? Like if they call and leave a vague message, or they keep changing what they want. I don’t want to miss the little traps. And if there’s a deadline in the notice, I’d rather have a simple way to track that too.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — keep it tight and don’t volunteer anything.

### Short reply
> I received your notice. I’m reviewing it and will respond in writing.

If you want it a little firmer:

> I received your notice. Please keep all communication in writing.

### What else to log
Besides the obvious stuff, keep track of:

- **Every call attempt**: date, time, number, who called if known
- **Voicemails**: save them or write down the exact message
- **Vague requests**: “send docs” is not enough — note what they asked for and when
- **Changing demands**: if they ask for one thing, then switch to another, write both down
- **Deadlines they mention**: even if it’s only said on the phone
- **Who said what**: name, company, title, and exact wording if you can
- **Missing attachments**: if they say they sent something but it never came through
- **Any contradictions**: different dates, different amounts, different reasons, different instructions

### Simple deadline tracker
Use a basic note or spreadsheet with:

- **Date received**
- **What they asked for**
- **Deadline given**
- **How they gave it**: letter, email, call, voicemail
- **What you sent back**
- **Date sent**
- **Proof of sending**: email copy, certified mail receipt, screenshot

### Good rule
If it was only said once and not in writing, treat it as **unconfirmed** until you get it in writing.

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page claim log template you can copy into Notes or a spreadsheet.

user_simulator.text:
That’s enough. Short reply works.

I received your notice. I’m reviewing it and will respond in writing.

If I had to make it a little harder:

I received your notice. Please keep all communication in writing.

And yeah, log the ugly little stuff too — every call, voicemail, half-baked request, and when they change the story. I’d also note:
- exact date and time
- who called or wrote
- what they asked for, word for word if possible
- whether they gave a deadline
- whether the deadline was only said on the phone
- if they said they sent something and it never showed up
- if the amount, date, or reason keeps changing

For deadlines, I’d keep one simple line per item:
- date received
- deadline given
- how it came in
- what you sent back
- date you sent it
- proof you sent it

Paper trail. That’s the whole game.
